name: Mae
classification_notes: Mae combines a generous x-height, open apertures and softly tapered curved strokes with humanistic qualities, placing it clearly in the humanist sans tradition despite its rational, text-optimised construction.
summary: A nine-weight humanist sans from Lettermin built around near-invisible readability, with true italics, variable font files and over a thousand glyphs.
description: Mae is designed around the principle that a typeface serves best when readers stop noticing it. Its wide counters, open apertures and softly tapered shoulders produce reliable legibility from small print on low-grade paper through to screen interfaces and ebooks. Nine weights run from Thin to Black, each paired with a true italic, and stylistic sets offer alternate forms for commonly confused characters including a slab-serif capital I, semi-serif lowercase i and l, and a rounded y.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Mae as a typeface built on near invisibility, designed to reduce visual friction so the message reaches the reader without interference. The family features a generous x-height, wide counters and open apertures, with humanistic qualities balanced against a rational appearance. Nine weights from Thin to Black are paired with true italics, supplemented by variable font files and an expansive character set of more than a thousand glyphs including circled numerals, currency symbols, arrows and pointing hands.

name: Mae Soft
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with fully rounded terminals and open apertures throughout, placing it firmly in the rounded sans subcategory distinct from conventional grotesque or humanist cuts.
summary: A rounded sans-serif in nine weights with full italics and variable font support, designed for warm, approachable branding work.
description: Released in December 2025 by Ermin Međedović, Mae Soft is the softer sibling of the Mae family, built around generous curves, rounded terminals and open apertures that lend it an organic, tactile quality. Nine weights from Thin to Black each carry matching italics, and the family ships as variable fonts covering both the upright and italic axes. The character set is broad, covering Latin-based languages with small caps, four figure styles, discretionary ligatures, circled numerals and a range of pictographic extras.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Mae Soft as smooth, rounded and extra creamy — a friendly sans-serif pitched against a world of sharp edges, with an organic quality suited to themes of nature, food and the simple pleasures of life. It is available in nine weights from Thin to Black, ships as a variable font, and includes an expansive character set with small caps, multiple figure styles, discretionary ligatures and circled numerals.

name: Enra Sans
classification_notes: Described as a hybrid of technical and human qualities, with a large x-height, open apertures and narrow proportions characteristic of humanist sans design. The selective use of serifs on I, J, i, j and l for legibility reinforces its functional, reader-oriented character.
summary: A humanist sans conceived to balance technical precision with warmth, available in eight weights with italics and a variable font, covering Latin Extended including Vietnamese.
description: Originally sketched in the early 2010s and substantially redrawn before release, Enra Sans sits within a three-member system alongside Enra Slab and Enra Semi, all sharing a common skeleton with harmonised weights. Its large x-height, generous counters and open apertures keep text readable at small sizes, while the squarish structure holds well on screen. Selective serifs on capital I, J and lowercase i, j and l aid disambiguation, with stylistic sets allowing their removal for a more conventional appearance.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Enra Sans as a child of the nineties, designed to appeal to a no-nonsense, tech-savvy audience without losing the personal connection type needs to make with readers. Work began in the early 2010s as an investigation into making a design feel simultaneously technical and human; after lying dormant for nearly a decade the typeface was redrawn with its hybrid character intact and its character set substantially expanded. Together with Enra Slab and Enra Semi, it forms what Lettermin call a power trio built on a shared skeleton.

name: Enra Slab
classification_notes: Enra Slab is a slab serif with humanist proportions, described as blending a technical structure with human warmth. The sturdy serifs add stability to letterforms drawn with a hybrid character rooted in 1990s utilitarian design.
summary: A utilitarian slab serif from the Enra type system, sharing a common skeleton with its sans and semi siblings across eight weights plus a variable axis.
description: Part of a three-member family alongside Enra Sans and Enra Semi, this slab variant adds sturdy serifs that reinforce the stability of letterforms designed for legibility across print and screen. A large x-height, open apertures and narrow proportions allow it to perform well at small sizes without feeling cramped. The character set is extensive, covering Vietnamese and a wide range of Latin languages, with small caps, multiple numeral sets, full superscript and subscript alphabets, and circled or cartridge figure variants via OpenType stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Enra as a child of the nineties, following the approach of durable typefaces from that decade that balance a technical, no-nonsense structure with a human connection. Work on the sans began in the early 2010s, lay dormant for nearly a decade, and was then redrawn with its hybrid character intact and its character set expanded. Enra Slab, Enra Sans and Enra Semi share a common skeleton and harmonised weights, forming what the foundry calls a power trio.

name: Enra Semi
classification_notes: Enra Semi is a hybrid sans that carries serifs only on the tops of letterforms, giving it a character distinct from both pure sans and full slab designs. This partial-serif approach places it in a semi-serif subcategory, combining the openness of a grotesque with structural anchoring from selective serifs.
summary: A semi-serif with top-only serifs that gives letterforms a forward-gliding quality, part of a three-sibling type system also including Enra Sans and Enra Slab.
description: The top-only serifs distinguish Enra Semi from its siblings by lending each letterform a sense of directional movement along the baseline. Designed around a large x-height, generous counters and open apertures, it holds up well at small sizes on screen and in print, with narrow proportions that conserve space without feeling tight. The family ships as 16 static styles plus a variable font, and includes a thorough OpenType feature set covering small caps, multiple numeral styles, circled figures via stylistic sets and full Vietnamese coverage.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Enra as a child of the nineties, following the playbook of popular typefaces from that decade that have become enduring fixtures in visual culture. Work on the sans began in the early 2010s as an investigation into designs that feel simultaneously technical and human, before lying dormant for roughly a decade and being redrawn with its hybrid structure preserved and its character set expanded. Enra Sans, Enra Slab and Enra Semi share a common skeleton with harmonised weights, functioning as a coordinated type system.

name: Flac 30
classification_notes: An ultra-compressed sans whose letterforms grow outward from a fixed Thin skeleton as weight increases, producing an unusual visual rhythm rooted in film poster and mid-century jazz record sleeve typography. The extreme compression and distinctive weight progression place it firmly in the display category.
summary: An ultra-compressed display sans in 30 styles, including a 'Retalic' variant, inspired by old film poster lettering and mid-century Blue Note Records sleeve design.
description: Starting from a fixed ultra-compressed Thin skeleton, each heavier weight grows the letterforms outward rather than inward, keeping counters consistent across the range and producing a silhouette unlike conventional weight progressions. The family draws on the visual language of old movie poster type and Reid Miles's Blue Note Records sleeves, and was later used in a newspaper weekend supplement redesign before reaching its definitive form in 2024. At 30 styles plus a variable font, it includes upright, italic and a distinctive Retalic cut across ten weights, with thirteen stylistic sets, small caps, and a broad Latin Extended character set.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Flac as having its roots in film marketing, beginning with an ultra-compressed Thin weight drawn from the type found on old movie posters. Growing the letterforms outward from a fixed skeleton to add weight gave the family its distinct appearance. Evolving beyond cinema, it drew further inspiration from mid-century cool jazz, bossa nova and the Blue Note Records album sleeves of Reid Miles. After use in a newspaper weekend supplement redesign, the definitive version was completed in summer 2024. The name is an acronym: Flac 30 stands for 'Four-Letter Acronym,' with the number 30 indicating it is the narrowest member of a planned coordinated system of widths named after cassette tape time markings.

name: Filac 30
classification_notes: Filac 30 is a display sans built around extreme vertical-to-horizontal stroke contrast, where weights grow outward along the vertical stems while horizontal parts remain thin throughout. Its origins in ultra-compressed film poster type and midcentury jazz record sleeve design place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A high-contrast display sans grown from ultra-compressed film poster lettering, with vertical stems that expand dramatically across ten weights while horizontals stay uniformly thin.
description: Beginning as Flac, a study in ultra-compressed type from old movie posters, the design eventually found a second logic: rather than expanding letterforms outward in all directions, weight is added exclusively along the vertical axis. The result is a ten-weight family with a striking stroke contrast that intensifies as the weights increase. The '30' in the name signals a planned typographic system of widths, with wider siblings at 45, 60, 90 and beyond yet to come. Stylistic references run from midcentury cool jazz to Blue Note Records sleeve design by Reid Miles.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Filac 30 as originating from an experiment with ultra-compressed film poster lettering. The defining idea arrived when the designer asked what would happen if the Thin weight gained mass only in the horizontal direction, leaving the thin horizontals unchanged and letting the vertical stems grow outward. The result is a series of ten weights with increasingly dramatic stroke contrast. The numeral 30 denotes it as the narrowest member of a larger planned system, with wider variants named 45, 60, 90 and so on, following the convention of cassette tape time markings.

name: Refer
classification_notes: An all-capitals typeface conceived specifically for crossword puzzles and tight-spaced print scenarios, with no true lowercase, placing it firmly in the display category with a functional, geometric sensibility.
summary: An all-capitals family originally designed for crossword puzzles, built around the diacritical demands of Slovenian and a duplexed structure that keeps line length fixed across weights.
description: Conceived for the constrained grid of crossword puzzles, Refer is composed entirely of capital letters engineered to occupy minimal vertical space while remaining legible. Diacritics are handled by lightening the upper portions of accented letters or, at tight line spacing, offering accented small caps whose total height never exceeds cap height. Three subfamilies address distinct print conditions: the plain Refer for clean digital and print use, Refer Print with ink traps for low-quality paper, and Refer Press with soft ink-flow irregularities that evoke traditional press printing.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Refer as a typeface that excels at a very specific task, originally conceived for crossword puzzles and composed entirely of capital letters for the tightest spaces. Designed primarily for Slovenian, it handles diacritical marks with two solutions depending on available vertical space. Three subfamilies cover clean digital and print use, challenging low-quality paper conditions via ink traps, and the warm, textured character of letterforms already printed on the page. All subfamilies are available as static and variable fonts with a duplexed structure so weight changes never alter line length.

name: Refer Print
classification_notes: An all-capitals display family with ink traps engineered for demanding print conditions, conceived originally for crossword puzzles and space-constrained layouts. Its purpose-built, single-case design places it firmly in the display category rather than a conventional text serif or sans-serif.
summary: An all-capitals family with ink traps, originally designed for crossword puzzles in Slovenian, engineered to hold sharp at small sizes on low-quality paper.
description: Refer Print is the ink-trap variant within the three-subfamily Refer collection, built for newspapers and other challenging print conditions where ink spread would otherwise compromise legibility. Every letterform is capitals-only, with accented characters handled by two strategies: lighter upper strokes on top-accented letters to reduce visual weight, and accented small caps that never exceed cap height for tight line spacing. The family uses a duplexed structure so weight changes do not alter line length, and all six static styles are matched by a variable font.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Refer Print as engineered for less-than-ideal printing conditions such as newspapers on low-quality paper, using ink traps to prevent ink spread and maintain sharpness. It is the second of three subfamilies in the Refer collection, all conceived from the original brief of a crossword-puzzle typeface for Slovenian, and all available as static and variable fonts with a duplexed weight axis.

name: Refer Press
classification_notes: An all-caps typeface with simulated press-printing irregularities, designed for spatial economy in crossword grids and tight editorial contexts. Its display orientation and deliberate textural distress place it firmly outside conventional text or sans-serif categories.
summary: An all-caps subfamily of the Refer collection that mimics the ink flow irregularities and soft blur of traditional press printing, originally engineered for crossword puzzle grids and Slovenian diacritics.
description: Refer Press is the third member of the Refer family, following the clean Refer and the ink-trap-equipped Refer Print. It introduces subtle ink flow irregularities and a softened, blurred quality that recalls letterforms already impressed on the page, giving a warm, organic texture while retaining the all-caps, space-conscious structure of its siblings. The duplexed design ensures weight changes do not alter line length, and accented small caps keep diacritics within cap height for tightly spaced settings. Available in six static weights and as a variable font.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Refer Press as a subfamily that captures the character of letterforms already printed on the page, with ink flow irregularities and a soft blurred effect evoking traditional press printing. Originally conceived for crossword puzzles and Slovenian diacritics, the family uses a duplexed structure so weight adjustments never affect line length, and offers accented small caps that stay within cap height for tight line spacing.

name: Ingra
classification_notes: Ingra is described explicitly as a humanist sans-serif with near-monolinear strokes, open counters, and horizontally/vertically aligned terminals — hallmarks of the humanist sans tradition, engineered toward text legibility.
summary: A near-monolinear humanist sans built around editorial practicality, offered in ten weights and three genuine widths with an extensive OpenType feature set.
description: Designed with editorial density in mind, Ingra keeps stroke contrast minimal and counters open, yielding a text colour denser than typical humanist sans-serifs — useful in narrow columns and tight leading. Three authentic widths, from Condensed to Wide, provide a genuine hierarchical palette rather than mechanically squeezed variants. The OpenType suite covers small caps, four numeral styles, superior and inferior figures, arbitrary fractions, ordinals, pictograms and 16 stylistic sets, making it a capable toolkit for complex information design.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Ingra as a typeface built around practical editorial needs, with a generous x-height, near-monolinear strokes, and three distinct widths crafted as full complements rather than afterthoughts. They highlight its role as a comprehensive typographic system for information design, covering everything from sustained body copy to numerical tables and infographic pictograms, and characterise its voice as one of quiet authority rather than expressive display.

name: Ingra Condensed
classification_notes: Ingra Condensed is described explicitly as an engineered humanist sans-serif with near-monolinear strokes, a generous x-height, and horizontally and vertically aligned terminals — hallmarks of the humanist sans genre.
summary: A condensed humanist sans with ten weights, designed as one of three purposefully drawn widths in a larger editorial type system.
description: Ingra Condensed is built for information density rather than creative expression, with near-monolinear strokes, a tall x-height, and cleanly aligned terminals that keep counters open at small sizes. It sits within a three-width family alongside Ingra Regular and Ingra Wide, with each width drawn as a distinct cut rather than a mechanical compression. OpenType support is thorough, covering small caps, four figure styles, superior and inferior numerals, ordinals, pictograms and an extensive range of stylistic alternates across sixteen sets.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Ingra as foremost an editorial typeface, built around sustained reading, information density and typographic precision. The condensed width is offered as a fully crafted complement to the regular and wide cuts, suited to dense labels and captions without sacrificing typographic integrity. The family functions, in their words, as a comprehensive toolkit for typographic information design rather than a purely expressive voice.

name: Ingra Wide
classification_notes: Ingra Wide is a humanist sans-serif with near-monolinear strokes, horizontally and vertically aligned terminals, and a generous x-height — characteristics placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition, engineered for editorial legibility rather than geometric abstraction.
summary: The wide-width member of the Ingra family, a near-monolinear humanist sans built for editorial systems requiring hierarchical range across weights and widths.
description: Ingra Wide spans nine weights from Hair to Extra Bold and is designed as a genuine complement to the standard and condensed widths of the Ingra family, rather than a mechanically stretched derivative. Strokes are nearly monolinear with terminals on horizontal and vertical axes, keeping counters open at small sizes while holding sufficient weight at display scale. OpenType support is thorough: small caps, four numeral styles, superior and inferior figures, ordinals, arbitrary fractions and sixteen stylistic sets covering alternate letterforms for a, g, i, k, l, u, y and more.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Ingra as a typeface built around practical editorial needs, with a generous x-height, a weight range from Hair to Extra Bold and three distinct widths crafted as full-fledged designs rather than interpolated compressions. They note that the near-monolinear stroke contrast and open counters maintain legible column grey in narrow measures and tight leading, and that the inclusion of pictograms, extensive figure variants and OpenType features positions Ingra as a toolkit for typographic information design rather than a single-use text face.

name: Dita
classification_notes: A low-contrast sans serif with rounded stroke terminals throughout, designed to function at both display and text sizes. Its construction follows conventional grotesque principles but is distinguished by consistent rounded endings across all weights.
summary: A rounded sans serif from Lettermin designed to hold up across both text and display settings, with consistent circular terminals maintained across five weights.
description: Designed by Ermin Međedović and published in 2018, this family extends to 30 fonts across three widths: standard, condensed and wide. The weight range is deliberately constrained from Light to Extra Bold, a decision driven by the difficulty of drawing rounded terminals at extreme stroke thicknesses. A generous x-height and spacious counters support readability at small sizes, and the family serves as the UI typeface for the Lettermin website itself. Each font includes five numeral variants, small caps and a set of stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Dita as a soft, rounded sans serif that combines the freshness of display type with the discipline of a text face. They note that the weight range was intentionally kept tight to preserve consistent rounded terminals across all styles, and that the full Dita Collection spans 30 fonts across condensed, normal and wide widths. Dita supports almost all European Latin-script languages and features rich OpenType functionality including five figure variants per numeral.

name: Dita Condensed
classification_notes: A low-contrast sans with rounded stroke terminals and geometric underpinnings; the rounded endings and even stroke weight place it firmly in the geometric humanist camp rather than grotesque.
summary: A condensed cut of Ermin Međedović's rounded geometric sans, offering five weights with italics and small caps across a generous 928-glyph character set.
description: Part of the 30-font Dita Collection alongside normal and wide widths, the Condensed family runs from Light to Extra Bold with matching italics in each weight. Rounded stroke terminals define the look, while a generous x-height and open counters keep lighter weights legible at text sizes and on screen. Numerals come in five variants covering proportional and tabular lining and oldstyle figures plus small-cap figures, making the family practical for editorial and data-heavy layouts.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Dita Condensed as a soft, rounded sans serif combining the freshness of display type with the discipline of a text face. They note that the weight range was deliberately restrained to keep stroke-rounding consistent across all styles, and point to the generous x-height and spacious counters as the details that allow lighter weights to hold up in body text and on-screen UI settings. Dita serves as the main UI typeface on the Lettermin website itself.

name: Dita Wide
classification_notes: A low-contrast rounded sans serif with geometric terminals and consistent stroke weights, placing it squarely in the geometric sans category with a softer, contemporary inflection.
summary: A wide-cut rounded sans serif with consistent low-contrast strokes, designed to work across both text and UI contexts.
description: Part of the 30-font Dita Collection alongside normal and condensed widths, the Wide cut spans five weights from Light to Extra Bold, each with a complementary italic and small caps. Rounded stroke terminals are maintained consistently across all weights, which required restraining the weight range to keep the rounding optically coherent. Figure variants include proportional and tabular lining and oldstyle figures, plus small-cap numerals, making the family well suited to editorial hierarchies and data-heavy layouts.
foundry_blurb: Lettermin describe Dita as a soft, rounded sans serif that combines the freshness of display type with the discipline of a text face, designed by Ermin Međedović and published in 2018. They note that its generous x-height and spacious counters keep the lighter weights readable at small sizes, and that Dita serves as the main UI typeface on the Lettermin website itself. The Dita Collection totals 30 fonts across three widths, with five weights and matching italics in each, along with small caps and five figure variants per style.
